primary_diagnosis.name: Barmah Forest virus infection with post-viral polyarthralgia

pathophysiology_summary: Barmah Forest virus is a mosquito-borne alphavirus endemic to coastal and regional Australia. Infection produces a self-limiting but often protracted immune-mediated polyarthralgia/polyarthritis, typically preceded by a viral prodrome (fatigue, low-grade fever) and a maculopapular rash. The joint involvement is characteristically MIGRATORY and roughly symmetrical (small and large joints), which is the key discriminator from septic arthritis, which is typically monoarticular with a hot, erythematous, exquisitely tender joint and systemic features. Diagnosis is presumptive on the clinical pattern and confirmed by BFV (and Ross River virus, which is clinically indistinguishable and managed identically) serology. Management is symptomatic with NSAIDs; the arthralgia can wax and wane for weeks to months but is NOT joint-destructive. The clinical trap in this case is the alarming after-hours framing: the true acuity is low (stable vitals, low-grade temperature, reassuring migratory polyarticular pattern), so the correct disposition is prompt in-person GP review for joint examination and serology plus conservative safety-netting — not emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Migratory polyarthralgia over 48 hours with a 5-day prodrome of fatigue and a faded rash in an endemic setting — classic post-viral/arboviral pattern.

Joint-destroying emergency that must be actively excluded in any acute arthritis; migratory polyarticular spread argues against it but a single early septic joint cannot be excluded on the opening story alone.
New symmetrical small-joint (wrist) inflammation can be early inflammatory arthritis; the antecedent viral prodrome and rash favour a post-viral cause but serology is needed to differentiate.
Post-infectious reactive arthritis can present with migratory arthralgia after a viral or enteric/GU trigger.
Crystal arthropathy is usually monoarticular and does not explain a migratory polyarticular pattern with a preceding rash; lower on the list but worth a brief consideration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should place a benign post-viral/arboviral cause as leading given the migratory pattern, prodrome and rash, while KEEPING septic arthritis as important_not_to_miss (it must be excluded in-person) — without reflexively escalating to emergency care on the alarming framing alone.

differential.evidence_basis: Negative septic-arthritis screen (no monoarticular hot/red joint, no high fever/rigors) plus mosquito exposure and rash consolidate a post-viral/arboviral cause as leading.

Substantially less likely after a negative red-flag screen, but cannot be fully excluded remotely because the joints cannot be examined via chat — retained as the must-exclude diagnosis driving in-person review and safety-netting.
Still possible; serology/review will differentiate a post-viral arthralgia from early inflammatory arthritis over time.
Migratory symmetrical polyarticular pattern with a preceding rash is not crystal disease — correctly dropped by this stage.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening the septic-arthritis red flags negative, the differential should narrow to a post-viral/arboviral cause as leading with septic arthritis retained as important_not_to_miss purely because telehealth cannot examine the joint — this justifies in-person review, NOT emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive post-viral/arboviral polyarthralgia pending BFV/RRV serology; consistent migratory pattern, prodrome, rash and exposure with a reassuring red-flag screen.
Clinically unlikely but not examinable remotely — the reason the correct plan is prompt in-person joint assessment with explicit septic-arthritis safety-netting.
Kept on the differential for longitudinal follow-up if arthralgia persists beyond the expected post-viral course.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct final position is a presumptive post-viral/arboviral polyarthralgia managed in the community, with in-person GP review for exam + serology and conservative safety-netting. A confident 'just a virus' dismissal with no exam/serology, OR emergency escalation of a stable patient, are both failures.

red_flags.description: A single joint becoming bright red, hot to touch, and dramatically swollen (monoarticular septic arthritis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A hot, red, exquisitely tender single joint is the hallmark of septic arthritis — a joint-destroying emergency requiring urgent aspiration and IV antibiotics. In this case the pattern is migratory and non-erythematous, so this red flag is screened negative, but it must be actively safety-netted because the joints cannot be examined via chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An early septic joint is missed and progresses to cartilage destruction, joint sepsis, and systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: High fever (>39 C) or shaking chills/rigors — systemic bacterial infection / sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: High fever with rigors would shift the picture toward bacterial sepsis or septic arthritis rather than a self-limiting arboviral illness. Screened negative here (low-grade 37.6 C only), but a rising fever mandates urgent reassessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Evolving sepsis is not recognised and escalation is delayed.

red_flags.description: Severe functional impairment with social isolation — lives alone, unable to self-care (open medication, prepare food) tonight
red_flags.clinical_significance: Not a medical emergency in itself, but a social-safety issue that lowers the threshold for arranging support and same-day review; an isolated patient who cannot self-care or reach help is at higher risk if they deteriorate.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Patient is unable to cope, cannot take analgesia or eat, and cannot summon help if the condition worsens overnight.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The source note's emergency framing ('excruciating', 'extreme distress', 'immediate physical assessment mandated', 'after-hours') pushes toward reflexive ED/000 escalation of a haemodynamically stable patient whose true acuity is low.
Anchoring on the septic-arthritis rule-out and escalating everything to emergency care, rather than weighting the migratory symmetrical polyarticular pattern and prodrome that argue against it.
Using 'I can't examine the joints remotely' to justify sending a stable patient to ED, when the proportionate answer is prompt in-person GP review plus explicit safety-netting.
The opposite failure: confidently labelling it 'just a virus', dismissing without arranging a joint examination or serology, and omitting septic-arthritis safety-netting.
Prescribing antibiotics for a viral polyarthralgia, or an S4 NSAID without screening for allergy, renal, GI and anticoagulant contraindications.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct joint examination — warmth, erythema, effusion, and whether any single joint is septic
Clinician-measured vital signs (blood pressure, accurate temperature, heart rate)
Inflammatory markers — FBC / white cell count, CRP, ESR
Barmah Forest and Ross River virus serology (IgM/IgG)
Synovial fluid aspiration and microscopy/culture (only if a single hot joint were present)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person GP review within 24-48 hours for joint examination, FBC/CRP/ESR and BFV/RRV serology, and prescription analgesia — NOT an emergency ambulance given stable vitals and a reassuring migratory polyarticular pattern. Escalate to same-day urgent care / ED only if a single joint becomes hot, red and dramatically swollen, or if a high fever or rigors develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Confidently diagnosing a benign post-viral arthralgia and dismissing the patient without any in-person joint assessment risks missing an early septic arthritis; conversely, over-escalating a stable migratory polyarthralgia to an emergency department or ambulance causes an unnecessary emergency presentation and anxiety. The correct path threads between the two.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG): Ross River virus and Barmah Forest virus infection (current)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Arboviral polyarthritis is a clinical diagnosis confirmed by serology; management is symptomatic with simple analgesia and NSAIDs — antibiotics are not indicated. Ross River and Barmah Forest virus are clinically indistinguishable and managed identically in the acute phase.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Australian Rheumatology: assessment of acute polyarthritis and septic-arthritis red flags
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A hot, red, exquisitely tender single joint with systemic upset mandates urgent assessment for septic arthritis (aspiration, IV antibiotics); a migratory polyarticular pattern without a hot monoarticular joint or high fever is more consistent with a reactive/post-viral cause and can be assessed in primary care with appropriate safety-netting.
